agent_raw: The promotion model's fairness claim cannot be supported by the evidence presented. The evaluation reports macro-F1 on historical promotion decisions — this measures how accurately the model matches historical outcomes, not whether it produces fair outcomes across gender groups. Fairness and accuracy are different properties. A model can achieve higher macro-F1 while simultaneously failing equal opportunity, increasing demographic disparity, or worsening calibration for underrepresented groups. The fact that the evaluation shows a single aggregate confusion matrix without any gender-stratified breakdown makes it impossible to assess fairness by any definition. Additionally, the training labels — prior manager recommendations approved or rejected by a committee — are not a neutral or validated fairness target. If the historical promotion process disadvantaged women through manager recommendation patterns, a model trained to predict those outcomes replicates the pattern more consistently, which is the opposite of fairness. Name and photo removal are sound practices for reducing direct use of identity signals, but they do not prevent proxy feature effects and do not constitute a fairness evaluation. Before any fairness claim can be made, the proposal needs: (1) a specified fairness criterion; (2) gender-stratified metrics on the test set; and (3) an audit of the historical training labels for gender disparity controlling for job level and observable performance.
all_issues_raised: No gender-stratified metrics or specified fairness criterion
Historical promotion labels may encode gender bias
